column1: Review whether each candidate document-type tag is supported by the descriptive text. Use the dropdowns in columns L–N, then add a short reason in Review Notes where useful.

column3: FRAM - Charles PINAUD | Charles PINAUD
column11: Charles PINAUD (1883-1964) was mobilized in 1914 and served four years of war. During the Battle of the Marne, he contracted typhoid. He also did Verdun. He's a terrific war photographer. An insurer by profession, he resumed this profession after the conflict. Married, he had three children (two girls and a boy)

column3: FRAM - Jean-Baptiste LE FLEM | Jean-Baptiste LE FLEM
column11: Jean-Baptiste LE FLEM (1889-1970) was mobilized on August 3, 1914 as a 2nd class in the 19th Infantry Regiment. He was taken prisoner on December 17, 1914. A linen tailor before the war, he resumed his trade at the end of the conflict.

column3: FRAD071-022 Justin et Claudien GONNET. | Document from Justin and Claudien Gonnet.
column11: Story by Michel PETIT about his great-uncle, Justin GONNET and his maternal grandfather, Claudien GONNET. Son of Jean Marie and Marie GONNET, née CHOPELIN, Claudien and Justin GONNET both participated in the First World War. Justin, born on August 6, 1885, worked as a servant. Matricule 57 of the 1905 class of the Mâcon office, he was successively assigned to the 17th, 109th and 152nd infantry regiments. Wounded by shrapnel in 1915 in Reillon (Meurthe-et-Moselle), Justin, proposed for medical reform, died of Addison's disease on March 22, 1918. His brother Claudien, born May 22, 1889, practiced the farming profession. Matricule 132 of the 1909 class of the Mâcon office, Claudien, was cited in the order of the 21st battalion of foot hunters (n° 529 of April 29, 1918) and was decorated with the Croix de guerre with bronze star.

column3: AMSM-020_1 : Jean Marcadal | Jean Marcadal
column11: _ I had to take many steps to obtain the archival elements concerning seven members of our family. Research of Jean Marcadal, farmer born on 8/6/1896 in Réghaïa Algeria, uncle of my wife. From the Regiment of Algerian Riflemen. Joined the front at the age of 19 in Verdun. 2 citations, a military medal. Died as a result of his wounds and buried in the necropolis of Amiens on 3/5/1918. When we repatriated from Algeria in 1962, we had no echo of him. Forgotten until 1998 when we learned the date and place of his burial. _a photo _1 description of the services requested.

column3: FRAM - Pierre Coeffic | Document about Pierre Coeffic
column11: My grandfather Pierre Coeffic was a farmer in Keryado (Morbihan) at the Kergoal farm. Sergeant in the 65th infantry regiment, class 1910, he made the Marne, Verdun the Somme and Belgium. Wounded and gassed he obtained a citation and 5 medals. Photograph of Pierre Coeffic and his 5 medals.

column3: FRAD008_13 Un sergent sauve son lieutenant, qui lui remet la légion d'honneur après-guerre | A sergent saves his lieutenant, who gives him the legion d'honneur after war
column11: Sergeant Marius Ancel, of the 170th infantry regiment, was born in 1896 in Golbey, in the Vosges. He worked as a game hunter. He was a very brave soldier, as evidenced by his many citations and decorations. The contributor, his grandson, remembers in particular that he saved his lieutenant. He also told her that he killed "his first German" with a bayonet. He began to cry shortly after because, having looked in his papers, he had noticed that he was the father of a family, and he himself was an orphan. When he retired, he came to live with his daughter in Revin (Ardennes). Photocopy of press article

column3: FRAM - Journal de guerre de Jean Carré | Document from Jean Corentin Carré
column11: Jean Corentin Carré, born in Faouët (Morbihan), on January 9, 1900. On April 27, 1915, he enlisted at the age of 15 in the 41st infantry regiment. He arrived at the front at 15 years and 6 months and was assigned to the 410 infantry regiment. Section leader at 17, he earned the ranks of corporal, sergeant and adjutant, citations to the division, the army corps and the army. He died in aerial combat at the age of 18 and 3 months, on March 18, 1918. He was the youngest death for France in Faouët. In order to be able to engage, he invents an identity. His name was then Auguste Duthoy, born in Rumigny (Ardennes), on April 10, 1897. On December 29, 1916, from the depths of the trenches, he wrote a letter to Colonel Treillard, commander of the 410th infantry regiment. He asks to resume his identity, without leaving the front. This diary is kept at the Jean Carré college in Faouët, inaugurated in 1978. Journal of Jean Carré Press cutting with the letter of Jean Carré, addressed to Colonel Treillard, published in the newspaper Le Petit Parisein on July 8, 1917

column3: FRBMBO-070 Histoire du soldat Albéric Bru | Albéric Bru's story
column11: Mr. Jean-Michel Bru bears witness to the memory of his paternal grandfather: Albéric Bru born in 1881 in Bergerac. He was called up at the age of 33 while teaching mathematics at Blaye College. Between the beginning of August 1914 and the end of September 1915, he wrote a war diary. He was assigned to the ambulance of the 92nd Territorial Infantry Division. Leaving Ile de France, he passed through the Oise, the Somme, the Pas de Calais, the Argonne. From September 20, 1915, he was assigned to Bar-le-Duc (Meuse) in the laboratory of the 3rd army as a chemist. He was in charge of the powder division (choking gases) He was demobilized in 1919. Document: Three small notebooks (97 pages in all) which constitute a war diary between September 1914 and September 1915 from which it was necessary to select extracts.

column3: Brief van Omer De Backer aan zijn vader, geschreven aan het front in Pervijze (12/11/1914) | Letter from Omer De Backer to his father, written from the front in Pervijze (12/11/1914)
column11: Pervyse, 12-11-14 My dear Father, I send you all my best wishes for health and happiness on your birthday. I have never, since my tenderest youth, ever written to you in circumstances so critical for all of us. I hope, despite the Prussian reinforcements which still arrive every day, you will not have to leave the house where you have spent the best part of your life; you may be among the privileged few who will never be Prussians. We are still victoriously holding our own all the way. The Kaiser however said "We must take Calais at all costs" and despite this formal order what happens? The Prussians are massacred en masse. We will not know how many Prussians died along the Nieuport-Arras line until after the war, but what is certain is that figure will be fantastic. I am always well. I haven't heard from you since I left Beveren. I don't even know if you get this letter. Don't worry if you don't receive my correspondence regularly because some get lost. Take care my dear parents brother and sister, and if everything goes as I hope, there will still be good days. My best kisses to you all. Omer I haven't heard from Michel or Remi. They are all in good health. Copy of a letter from Omer de Backer to his father Leopold (constable in Beveren aan de Yser) from the front in Pervijze on 12 November 1914 on the occasion of his name day (15 November)

column3: FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story
column11: House painter, Marcel Jean Eugène Migot is the son of Victor Edouard Migot and Clémence Anne Fougerol. Married to Germaine Berthe Filon, he has a daughter, Nicole Migot. Called on April 12, 1915 in Vannes (Meucon camp), he volunteered to join the front with the 116th infantry regiment, within the 29th Company. He was hospitalized twice: the first time, in Toulouse, from September 16 to December 20, 1916, following a concussion, and the second time, in Chateaudun, for a wound made in the thorax, when he fought at the Chemin des Ladies. Although he was seriously injured, he nevertheless returned to the front in 1918, once his convalescence was complete. His courage is rewarded with numerous decorations: Legion of Honor, military medal, war cross with palms (2 citations), combatant's cross, Verdun medal, Saint-Mihiel medal, medal of the Survivors of Aisne, medal of the wounded, silver and bronze medal of the voluntary combatants. After his return from the front, he wrote a lot about the war. Some of his texts are kept in the municipal archives of Saint-Denis. Others have been published, in particular Le Ravin de Veaudoine (in: Journal of the voluntary combatants). He also participated in the collective work Verdun: history of the fights which were fought from 1914 to 1918, on both banks of the Meuse, directed by Jacques Péricard and published in 1933. - Photographs - "Logbook" = story between 1915 and 1918 (one of which was published in a newspaper) - Certificate of good conduct - Volunteer combatant card - Identification and services report - Copy of a clinical "report"

column3: FRBMLY-66 Histoire de mes grands parents Henri et Marie-Louise Mermin | Story of my grandparents
column11: Henri Mermin and Marie-Louise are from the same village in Haute-Savoie. Henri did his military service in Bizerte (Tunisia) in the 3rd Zouaves Regiment, then in 1913 he returned to France and worked at the Hôtel Moderne in Paris. He corresponded with Marie-Louise until March when he returned to his village, then they married in May 1914. He was called up in August 1914, and continued to correspond with his family and his wife, who was pregnant at the time. of their 1st child who was born on March 5, 1915. Henri Mermin was killed in November 1914 in battle at Tracy le Val. Correspondence between Henri, his wife and his family, photograph of the wedding of Henri and Marie-Louise and official telegram announcing his death.

column3: FRAD033-074 Histoire de Georges Coignet | Documents from Georges Coignet
column11: Georges Coignet was born on June 11, 1894 in Menon (Nièvre). He went to the front with his five brothers (Georges, Gaston, Henri, Jean and Georges) on October 18, 1914; they will all come back alive in 1918. Assigned to the 121st infantry corps, Georges Coignet is cited in the orders of regiment 335 on November 8, 1916 for having chased the enemy from a trench with grenades. He received the Croix de Guerre with bronze star. He was demobilized on September 10, 1919. Driver at "Esso Rep" in Bègles, he died in Villenave d'Ornon in November 1971. Military booklet accompanied by its identification plate. 9 black and white photographs and postcards (1914-1918).

column3: Kriegserinnerungen | Wartime memories
column11: War memories 1914-1919, printed guide through the exhibition "The Front" organized by frontline fighters in Berlin in 1919, photo album with pictures of a soldier in the Leipzig Train Replacement Department, 1st Esk. from various locations including the western front

column3: Schilderij van de kerk van Grimde | Grimde
column11: Painting representing the church of Grimde (Tienen). Inscription: "Aux soldats Belges tombés pour la patrie le 18 août 1914 reposant dans cette église de Grimde". The church is now a cemetery, the crypt contains 140 Belgian soldiers, victims of the Battle of Sint-Margriete-Houtem (18/8/1914). Painting of the church of Grimde.

column3: FRBMBX-056/ brothers in war | Brothers Petit in war
column11: Three brothers go to war Grandfather Numa Petit Great uncles: Guillaume Petit, Numa's brother and Gilbert Arnaud, their half-brother Numa was a nurse at 144 RI with the Tunisian spais in Charlerois. He did Verdun and the Marne. One day when he was supposed to go on leave, it was cancelled. In anger he threw away his tobacco and proclaimed "I'll never smoke again, I'll be sick of it". He will emerge unscathed, uninjured, from the war. Doc 1 photo of Numa Petit, photo of cigarette case and compass Doc 2-4 photo of Guillaume, military medals, diploma

column3: FRAD008_3 Documents trouvés par M. Bonalda | Documents found by M. BONALDA
column11: Mr. Bonalda found these documents in the bins of a sorting center for the most part, and in an attic for the last, between 2003 and 2004. He has no connection with the persons concerned but he has decided to keep these testimonies of the Great War for their historical value and for their link with the Ardennes. He is indeed passionate about history and a collector. In addition, some documents relate to his place of residence. He thought it was a shame to find such documents in trash cans. Notes from Abbé BOUCHER, from Issancourt. Entry of the Germans into Mohon, by Pierre FAUVILLE. Story by Adolphe OUICE, reservist at the 247th line. Name of the Germans staying in Charleville, with their address. Response letter from the King of Spain to the request for the release of Marcel DROUART.

column3: FRBNBU-163 Francisque Majesté porté disparu | Documents about Francisque Majesté
column11: Francisque Louis Majesté (paternal grandfather of the contributor) originally from a family of distillers from Ornans(25) was incorporated into the 94th infantry regiment (registration number 04518 and 5191) as quartermaster sergeant. Before the war, he worked as an accountant. From September 19, 1914, his family received no further news from him. He will be reported missing in Baconnes (Marne) until in 1919 one of his comrades confirms to his wife his injury and his disappearance. His body was never found. - Official documents - Letters

column3: FRAM - Martial Londrade, sergent au 92e territorial | Martial Londrade
column11: Martial Londrade's war diary and excerpt from an article from the gymnastics society "La Tulliste": the sergeant of the 92nd territorial, offered himself voluntarily, on February 23, 1916, to perform, under a violent bombardment (1st attack de Verdun) one of the most urgent tasks he was able to bring to a successful conclusion, showing great composure and a deep contempt for danger.

column3: FRAD058_021 M;Louis TOLLET, M. Etienne NOLOT | Documents from Mr Guy François Nolot
column11: Mr. Guy François NOLOT lent documents concerning soldiers Louis TOLLET and Etienne NOLOT. - Various documents of Louis TOLLET: diploma, decorations, medals. - A Louis TOLLET military booklet, a combatant's notebook, a mobilization booklet, a family booklet, quotations. - A family booklet of Etienne NOLOT, his military booklet, his mobilization booklet, and the order of call under the flags.

column3: FRAD058_042 M. Abel BLOND, M. Marius ARBLAY. | Documents from Mr Labbe
column11: Mr. Pierre LABBE lent documents concerning the soldiers: Abel Blond and Marius Arblay. - Postcards, a photograph of Abel Blond and two notifications as well as a file of the 29th infantry regiment. - Then: the identity card of soldier Abel Blond, his active membership card, his military card, his correspondence and a military brooch. - A general order sheet belonging to soldier Marius Arblay, his 2 active membership cards, 8 postcards, a "historical booklet of the 327th Infantry Regiment", two citations and a 1914-1918 poster.

column3: FRBMAN-012 Antoine Laurenty | Antoine Laurenty's story
column11: Antoine Laurenty, a Belgian citizen residing in Brussels, was in his fifties when the Great War broke out. He refused to leave his town and remained there throughout the conflict, despite the dangers and hardships. He began on August 1, 1914 a diary in which he related until his forces abandoned him in 1917 the daily life in the rear, made up of rumors and fleeting hopes. He regularly mentions his nephew René Sibenaler who is fighting in the trenches of the Yser. An edition of this journal was produced by Jean Sibenaler in 2003 at Cheminements editions in Angers under the title "The notebooks of a Belgian citizen, 1914-1918". This book is kept at the Municipal Library of Angers in the collections of the Printer's Legal Deposit under the symbol DLE B54292. - 2 linen-backed notebooks constituting Antoine Laurenty's diary - 1 digitization of the cover, prefaces, foreword and post-face of the 2003 edition of Antoine Laurenty's notebooks - 1 photograph of Antoine Laurenty, his wife Mathilde and her sister-in-law - 1 photograph of René Sibenaler in the trenches of the Yser - 10 small photographs concerning René Sibenaler at the front
